content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Asistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement
(916) 322-5662         322-5660           322-5901          322-6441

July 22, 1985


Senator James R. Mills
119 Thirteenth Street
Del Mar, CA 92014

Re: Advice Letter No. A-85-161

Dear Senator Mills:

Commission Chairman Dan Stanford has referred your letter
of July 15, 1985 to me. You have asked whether it is legal for
you to donate campaign funds to the San Diego Historical
Society, knowing that they would be used to publish a book from
which you may eventually receive royalties.

The Political Reform Act only deals with campaign finance
reporting; it does not determine whether particular uses of
campaign funds are legal or illegal. There is a prohibition of
"personal use" of campaign funds in the Elections Code (Sections
12400, et seq.). However, the Commission does not interpret or
enforce this "personal use" law. Only the Attorney General can
give opinions interpreting these sections of the Elections Code.

I enclose a copy of Elections Code Sections 12400-12407 for
your information. I am sorry we cannot be of more assistance.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
General Counsel

BAM:plh
Enclosure

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • •    Administration • •    Executive/Legal • •    Enforcement
(916) 322-5662                322-5660                322-5901                322-6441


July 18, 1985


James R. Mills
119 Thirteenth Street
Del Mar, CA 92014

Dear Jim:

I am in receipt of your letter to me, dated July 15,
1985, requesting advice regarding the expenditure of campaign
funds to publish a history of San Diego through reprints of
articles from San Diego Magazine.

I have forwarded your letter to Barbara Milman, General
Counsel and Chief of our Legal Division for her review. And,
I can assure you that your inquiry will receive her prompt
attention.

If I can be of any further assistance, please do not
hesitate to contact me.

Very truly yours,

Dan Stanford
Chairman

DS:jc

JUL 17 1985


119 Thirteenth Street
Del Mar, California 92014
July 15, 1985


Dan Stanford, Chairman
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Post Office Box 507
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Stanford:

Over the course of a number of years I wrote dozens of articles
on the history of San Diego for San Diego Magazine. They would
make an interesting book, if collected and published together,
I think.

The San Diego Historical Society would, I believe, undertake
publishing such a book, but does not have the funds to do so.
If I were to contribute, from campaign funds, enough money to
set up a publication fund for works of history relating to this
area, and if the Society decided to publish my articles as a
book, and if I were to receive royalties on the sale of that
book, would that be a legal and approved use of campaign funds?

I would not, under those circumstances, be receiving money
myself from campaign funds. On the other hand, the moneys I
would receive would have been generated by the expenditure of
campaign funds.

Thank you for your assistance with this matter.

Sincerely,

[signature]
JIM MILLS

sections.question: You have asked whether it is legal for you to donate campaign funds to the San Diego Historical Society, knowing that they would be used to publish a book from which you may eventually receive royalties.
sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act only deals with campaign finance reporting; it does not determine whether particular uses of campaign funds are legal or illegal. There is a prohibition of "personal use" of campaign funds in the Elections Code (Sections 12400, et seq.). However, the Commission does not interpret or enforce this "personal use" law. Only the Attorney General can give opinions interpreting these sections of the Elections Code.
sections.question_synthetic: May a state senator donate campaign funds to the San Diego Historical Society for publication of a book from which the senator may receive future royalties?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC cannot provide advice on this matter because it only addresses campaign finance reporting under the Political Reform Act. Questions regarding the legality of personal use of campaign funds under the Elections Code must be directed to the California Attorney General.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes two pages: an initial routing letter from Chairman Dan Stanford (July 18, 1985) and the substantive response letter from General Counsel Barbara Milman (July 22, 1985). Response letter contains clear question and conclusion sections. This is a declination letter indicating the FPPC cannot provide the requested advice due to lack of jurisdiction.

QUESTION: You have asked whether it is legal for you to donate campaign funds to the San Diego Historical Society, knowing that they would be used to publish a book from which you may eventually receive royalties.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act only deals with campaign finance reporting; it does not determine whether particular uses of campaign funds are legal or illegal. There is a prohibition of "personal use" of campaign funds in the Elections Code (Sections 12400, et seq.). However, the Commission does not interpret or enforce this "personal use" law. Only the Attorney General can give opinions interpreting these sections of the Elections Code.

embedding.summary: Senator James R. Mills inquired whether he could donate campaign funds to the San Diego Historical Society for a book publication from which he might receive royalties. The FPPC General Counsel declined to answer, explaining that the FPPC lacks jurisdiction over personal use of campaign funds issues, which fall under the Elections Code and are enforced by the Attorney General.
